coredata.dc:title: Chemical analysis and identification the fluorophores of photoluminescent carbon dots beyond infrared and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Peng
Xiong, Yuan
Lei, Chen
Li, Yuan
Liu, Xiang
Zhang, Cheng
Sun, Yudie
Zhang, Jian
Lee, Chun-Sing
Zhang, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots or Carbon quantum dots (herein after abbreviated as CDs) with bight luminescence, derived from carbohydrates such as citric acid, glucose or sucrose, have been widely reported in recent publications. However, as to which functional groups and structures in CDs are responsible for luminescence, most explanations in current references are normally based on assumptions, instead of being convinced by the accurate information resting on experimental facts. Herein, we conducted an experimental study targeting at finding the fluorophores of CDs prepared from citric acid and glycine by fluorescent quenching. The experimental results revealed that the essence of fluorescence was manipulated by fluorophores of conjugated CC double bonds and aldehyde groups. The destruction of aldehyde groups or CC double bonds in the CDs led to a complete decay of luminescence. In addition, the results of this experiment were similar to those of CDs prepared from the sole citric acid, glucose or sucrose, which predicted the analogy of the CDs in chemical structures. Such information about fluorophore of CDs was valuable for further exploring more extensive application of CDs in sense technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorophores
Aldehyde
CC double bond
Fluorescent quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent responses of CDs for gradual additions of NaClO and KMnO4
Fluorescent responses of CDs for aniline, NaHSO3 and Fehling's solution as well as the reaction between CDs and Fehling's solution
The fluorescent responses of CDs for dopamine and folic acid
Fluorescent quenching of CDs by Fe3+
Fluorescent responses of CDs for NaClO and KMnO4
Fluorescent responses of CDs for aniline, Fehling's solution and NaHSO3 respectively
Analyses on the IR and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra of CDs
Fluorescent response of CDs for dopamine or folic acid
Analyses on fluorescent quenching of CDs by Fe3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22106005 , 21976002 , 22004003 ) and Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars ( 2008085J11 ). This work acknowledges the fellowship award from the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong SAR, China ( CityU PDFS2021-1S06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent quenching of the CDs by KMnO4, NaClO, aniline, Fehling's solution and NaHSO3 as well as the deduction of the fluorophores.

(a) UV–visible spectra of CDs and the changes by additions of 50 and 100 μL NaClO (0.6 mol L−1); (b) PL spectra of CDs upon additions of varied amounts of NaClO (0.6 mmol L−1); (c) PL spectra of CDs affected by additions of varied amounts of KMnO4 (1.5 mmol L−1). Insets of (b) and (c) exhibit images of the quenched solutions.

Schematic description on π and π* molecular orbitals as well as n orbital in a conjugated species; (a) Interaction between π molecular orbitals of a conjugated CC in CDs and the one of CO; (b) Such interaction between π molecular orbitals of a single CC bond and the one of CO.

(a) Fluorescent response of CDs for dropwise additions of aniline (1.0 mol L−1); (b) Variations of UV–visible spectra of CDs caused by additions of aniline (1.0 mol L−1); (c) Fluorescent responses of CDs for additions of Fehling's solution; (d) such responses for gradual addition of NaHSO3 (200 mg mL−1). Inset of (c) is the photographs of the Fehling's solution in test tube mixed with CDs; Insets of (d) demonstrate the fluorescent quenching of CDs solution by NaHSO3.
(a) IR spectra (2000-600 cm−1) and (b) C 1s signals of XPS of CDs.
Fluorescent responses of CDs for dropwise additions of dopamine (0.26 mol mL−1) and folic acid (1.13 mmol mL−1).

(a) Fluorescent quenching of CDs by gradual additions of Fe3+ (5.0 mmol L−1); (b) UV–visible spectra of CDs affected by 6 μL Fe3+ solutions (0.05 mol L−1); (c) Partly recovered fluorescence of CDs by EDAT-Na4 (0.4 mol L−1).

head.ce:title: Chemical analysis and identification the fluorophores of photoluminescent carbon dots beyond infrared and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma'anshan, Anhui 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan, Anhui 243032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Center of Super-Diamond and Advanced Films (COSDAF), City University of Hong Kong, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Center of Super-Diamond and Advanced Films (COSDAF)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Center of Super-Diamond and Advanced Films (COSDAF), City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong S.A.R., China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots or Carbon quantum dots (herein after abbreviated as CDs) with bight luminescence, derived from carbohydrates such as citric acid, glucose or sucrose, have been widely reported in recent publications. However, as to which functional groups and structures in CDs are responsible for luminescence, most explanations in current references are normally based on assumptions, instead of being convinced by the accurate information resting on experimental facts. Herein, we conducted an experimental study targeting at finding the fluorophores of CDs prepared from citric acid and glycine by fluorescent quenching. The experimental results revealed that the essence of fluorescence was manipulated by fluorophores of conjugated CC double bonds and aldehyde groups. The destruction of aldehyde groups or CC double bonds in the CDs led to a complete decay of luminescence. In addition, the results of this experiment were similar to those of CDs prepared from the sole citric acid, glucose or sucrose, which predicted the analogy of the CDs in chemical structures. Such information about fluorophore of CDs was valuable for further exploring more extensive application of CDs in sense technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: Various carbohydrates [1–4] or natural substances [5–7] are the common resources for preparing carbon dots or carbonquantum dots (CDs) since they provide plentiful functional groups involving in various reactions. In recent decades, CDs derived from citric acid [8–13] have widely appeared in various academic studies and discussions, which should be attributed to the dense functional groups of citric acid (e.g., carboxyl). The photoluminescence (PL) performance of CDs is most important in applications and has been a focus of extensive research interest. But for delving into the mechanism of PL, it is imperative to understand: which fluorophores of CDs are responsible for the PL. Among the functional groups of CDs, some are inherent to the raw materials [14,15]. For instance, the primary amino groups of starting materials are retained during the production of CDs [16,17]. Therefore, the CDs derived from citric acid are acidic. The starting materials of preparing CDs are generally none of the fluorescence, which indicates that the PL performance should be associated with the newly emerged groups during the carbonization, relevant with PL performance of CDs. But what are the new functional groups in CDs? And, the conjugated species are commonly proposed to explain the PL [18–20], but what type of chemical bond constitutes conjugation? On the surface, not all the conjugations can cause fluorescence.

Since CDs are mixtures, accurate information about the chemical structure thereof cannot be obtained. Hydrothermal treatment under violent conditions can cause complex reactions among raw materials. The hydroxyl or carboxyl group of carbohydrate might involve in esterification, etherification, elimination, and other reactions, and intermolecular assembly can be realized through carbonization. Under such a violent atmosphere, these functional groups may also be decomposed or oxidized. It is generally believed that carbonization not only arouses the generation of nanoparticles with different molecular weight distributions but also triggers the production of conjugated species within a nanoparticle. Meanwhile, in several published articles, some conjugated CC species were depicted schematically [1,13,21–23] though the real structure is in all likelihood even more complicated.

Until today, in most cases, the analyses on the chemical structure of CDs have relied mainly on infrared (IR) and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra (XPS). However, such measurement fails to actually provide accurate or complete information. The IR signal overlap of some functional groups in CDs usually makes it difficult to distinguish, especially in the range of 1600–1800 cm−1 [1,19,24], because the absorption may be caused by the overlap of the characteristic peaks of CC and CO. The CO signal may come from carbonyl, aldehyde, ester group or amide. Wavenumbers over 3000 cm−1 [1,19] might be the overlapping of hydroxyls from alcohol or carboxyl groups. Similarly, XPS of C 1s with high-resolution also gives the mixed signals of elemental C. The C-contained groups are judged by deconvolution, and because of the difficulty of discerning the variation of the binding energy of C element, it is not very accurate. To illustrate, the binding energy of C–O of CDs obtained by fitting operations are different in several publications (285.5 eV [25], 285.8 eV [26], 286.2 eV [5], etc.). In this case, IR or XPS measurement merely provides a mixed signal, which has been a troublesome problem for many researchers.

Most references described the applications of CDs in the detection of metallic ions [5,27,28] or biomolecules [27,29,30] through fluorescent quenching. This fluorescent response should be attributed to the reaction between the fluorophore of CDs and the target analyte, which further provides some valuable information about the underlying causes behind the PL. I believe that the quenching is linked to the interaction between the fluorophore and the target analyte. But it has been unknown that which fluorophore is destroyed and causes the quenching. If the answer to this unknown question can be acquired by experimental demonstrations combined with spectra analyses, the root causes of PL can be understood accordingly, that little open literature has concerned these issues until now.

Considering that, our study set out to find the fluorophores of CDs through fluorescent quenching, hoping to provide a basic understanding of the PL mechanism of CDs. The quenching should be due to the chemical response of CDs to the target analyte. Based on this interaction, the fluorophores or luminescent skeleton of CDs as shown in Fig. 1 was proposed schematically.

Citric acid monohydrate (99.5%), glycine (≥99.0%), aniline (≥99.5%), copper sulfate pentahydrate (99%), sodium hydroxide (97%), potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (99%), sodium bisulfite (≥58.5% by SO2), folic acid (≥98%), tetrasodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate tetrahydrate (>98.0%), and dopamine hydrochloride (98%) were purchased from Aladdin company (Shanghai, China). Sodium hypochlorite pentahydrate was purchased from TCI Company (Shanghai, China).

The CDs were prepared using the following technique: first, the citric acid and glycine solutions were pyrolyzed at 150 °C for 60 min [31]; afterwards, mixing the cooled product with 20 mL tetrahydrofuran and stirring well to ensure that the fluorescent material was completely dissolved in the solvent; then CDs were obtained by removing the solvent.

Fehling's solution was obtained by mixing solution 1 and solution 2 in equal volume. Solution 1 was prepared by dissolving 12.5 g sodium hydroxide (96%) and 13.7 g potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (99%) in 50 mL of water while Solution 2 was a solution of 3.45 g copper sulfate pentahydrate in 50 mL water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses of CDs for gradual additions of NaClO and KMnO4
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the fluorescent spectrum of CDs solution was measured (the concentrations of CDs was as high as possible to obtain higher intensity). Then the NaClO solution (0.6 mmol L−1, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10… and 200 μL respectively) was gradually added dropwise into CDs solution, during which time the fluorescent responses were recorded (excitation = 340 nm). A similar measurement was performed on the CD solution using KMnO4 (1.5 mmol L−1, 5, 10, 20, 30… and 200 μL respectively). The color of KMnO4 faded due to the addition of CDs solution, and was recorded with video to further prove the double CC bond in CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses of CDs for aniline, NaHSO3 and Fehling's solution as well as the reaction between CDs and Fehling's solution
ce:section.ce:para: To begin with, the fluorescent spectrum of CDs solution was measured. After that, the aniline dissolved in ethanol (1.0 mol L−1, 2.5, 5, 7.5… and 200 μL respectively) was added into the CDs solution, with a record of the fluorescent spectra (excitation = 340 nm). To detect the fluorescence response of NaHSO3 (200 mg mL−1, 20, 40, 60… and 200 μL, respectively), similar measurements were performed. The fluorescent response of Fehling's solution was also checked.

The reaction between CDs and Fehling's solution was carried out to witness the aldehyde groups of CDs. Specifically, 1 mL Fehling's solution was measured and mixed with 1 mL CDs solution (1.8 mg mL−1) in a test tube with a vibration to mix thoroughly. Next, that test tube was placed in boiling water for more than 30 min, and checking whether there was erythrin precipitate at the bottom of the test tube. The fluorescent response for Fehling's solution was recorded in video, which was used to dynamically observe the fluorescent quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescent responses of CDs for dopamine and folic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the experimental results described in 2.3, we inferred that the amino group of dopamine or folic acid should be reactive to the aldehyde group of CDs; the consumption of aldehyde groups might inhibit CDs fluorescence. Therefore, dopamine (0.26 mol mL−1, 2.5, 5, 7.5… and 200 μL respectively) or folic acid (1.13 mmol mL−1, 5, 10, 15… and 400 μL respectively) solutions were added to the CDs solution and the fluorescent responses were recorded (excitation = 340 nm) to prove our inference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent quenching of CDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: FeCl3 solutions (5.0 mmol L−1, 2.5, 5, 7.5… and 200 μL respectively) were added dropwise to the CDs solutions to investigate whether there is fluorescent quenching (excitation = 340 nm). If the quenching occurs, it should be attributed to the coordination between the CDs and Fe3+. To prove this, the tetrasodium ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA-Na4) solutions (0.4 mol L−1) were added into the quenched solution capture the coordinated Fe3+ (due to the more powerful coordination capability of EDTA-Na4); if the PL intensity is recovered, it supports our conclusion that quenching is caused by the coordination between Fe3+ and the CDs.

When the mixture of citric acid and glycine is pyrolyzed, a series of complicated reactions are triggered. Carbonation is accompanied by possible decomposition, elimination, oxidation, combination reactions, etc. until CDs are produced [31]. In Fig. 2 (a), the absorption at 250 and 347 nm comes from π→π* and n→π* transmissions respectively [32]. The former implies the double bond of CC in CDs whilst the latter is due to the double bond containing hetero atoms of oxygen (CO). The peak at 250 nm declares that the energy level difference from π to π* is reduced, the π energy level is higher and the π* energy level is lower. For a single CC, this difference is greater than that of the conjugated CC, as shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b). Thus, the red shift occurs for π→π* transmissions compared with the normal CC. When the conjugated CC and CO form a larger conjugate, the energy difference of n→π* transmission decreases since the enhanced energy level of π and decreased energy level of π* of the conjugated CC. This led to a significant red shift (347 nm) compared to the normal n→π* described in Fig. 3(b). CDs solution emitted bright blue fluorescence in aqueous medium. But what is unknown is which groups in CD are responsible for the fluorescence of CD?

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses of CDs for NaClO and KMnO4
ce:section.ce:para: As aforementioned in the Introduction sector, CDs are employed frequently in fluorescent analysis. Most of the time, this depends on the effect of the detected molecule or metallic cation on the fluorescence quenching. Quenching should be caused by the reaction with the fluorophore of CD; thereby, fluorescent quenching provides a potential approach to identify the fluorescent core of CDs.

The fluorescence spectrum of CDs is exhibited in Fig. S1 of Supporting Information. The fluorescent responses of CDs to NaClO are shown in Fig. 2(b). With the addition of NaClO, the intensity decreased gradually, which illustrates the potential application of CDs in detecting hypochloric acid or chlorine in aqueous media. To date, several experiments on the fluorescent quenching by hypochlorite ions have been reported [33–35], in which the reason for the quenching was ascribed to oxidation of hypochloric acid. But it is worth thinking about, which sites of CD were oxidized? Regrettably, this problem has not been solved yet in extant literatures [33–37]. Some people have proposed that oxidation will destroy the conjugation in the CDs or break the conjugated ring while others considered that the reductive groups of CDs are oxidized by hypochloric acid. Precisely, Fig. 2(c) also supports that the oxidation of CD leads to fluorescence quenching, and gradually adding KMnO4 causes a gradual decrease in PL intensity. The inset of Fig. 2(b) or (c) shows the obvious change in fluorescence when NaClO or KMnO4 solution is added. Besides, the video records the fading process of KMnO4, through which the rapid reaction between KMnO4 and CDs was evidenced.

Also, Fig. 2(a) reveals that when 50 and 100 μL NaClO was added, the UV–visible spectrum of CDs at the peak of 347 nm changed significantly. The resulted hypochloric acid destroys the conjugated CC bonds that result in an increase in the energy level of π* described in Fig. 3(b). Therefore, the original n→π* transmission (347 nm) disappeared after adding 100 μL NaClO. Oxidation of CC bonds of CDs should be the real cause of fluorescent quenching in this UV–visible spectroscopy analysis. From the opposite point of view, the conjugated CC bonds are the basic species in CDs and constitute the basic platform for light absorbing and emission.

The CC bonds of CDs is frequently cited in many references [13,19,21], which serves as a vital component of the conjugated species of CDs. So it is acceptable for CDs to be imaged as an aggregation of large amount of CC bonds. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the intensity has dropped by nearly 80%. The inset of Fig. 2(b) also exhibits complete quenching (the fluorescent quantum yield of original CDs is 59.8% [31]). This indicates that the conjugated CC bonds in CDs contributes the most to the fluorescent quantum yield. Additionally, Fig. 2(c) discloses that the fluorescence disappeared more completely than that of Fig. 2(b) which is primarily because of the more intensive oxidation of KMnO4 than hypochlorous acid.

Looking back at the previous literature and publications, it is not difficult to find that CDs are depicted schematically with a mixture of the nanoparticle. The particles are composed of fused rings with CC conjugated species [13,19,21]. The fluorescent quenching of NaClO and KMnO4, as well as the fading of KMnO4 solution by CDs provide strong evidence for the CC bonds in CDs. The quenching is due to the wrecking of the fluorophore of CC bonds in CDs. Intensive oxidizer of KMnO4 or NaClO destroys the CC bonds that induces decay of fluorescence of CDs. Such simple experiments have achieved an efficient way of identifying the fluorophore of CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responses of CDs for aniline, Fehling's solution and NaHSO3 respectively
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of CDs is also sensitive to aniline, evident in Fig. 4 (a). The added aniline almost completely quenches the fluorescence. The UV–visible spectra of Fig. 4(b) disclose that the peak of CDs at 347 nm was significantly weakened as the sight shifted. Aniline of 900 μL severely disrupted the absorption. These predicted the reaction between aniline and CDs. But which group in CDs reacts with the unique amino group of aniline? There is a possibility that aldehyde groups may be present in CDs. If true, CDs should be reactive with Fehling's solution.

It has been proved by our previous studies that even if the carboxyl was converted to the active ester of N-hydroxysuccinimide. fluorescence still existed [31,38]. This indicates that the carboxyl of the CDs is not contained in the fluorescence core. It can be seen that the fluorescent quenching in Fig. 4(a) should be due to the interaction between the aldehyde of CDs and the amino of aniline, not the carboxyl.

Obviously, erythrine precipitates are generated at the bottom of the test tube shown in the inset of Fig. 4(c). That is because the aldehyde of CDs reduces Cu2+ of Fehling's solution resulting in production of Cu2O. This reaction clearly demonstrates the aldehyde group of CDs. Fehling's solution also affects the fluorescence remarkably, see Fig. 4(c). It causes an almost complete decrease in fluorescence. Fehling's solution of 50 μL quenches the fluorescence by an estimated 80%. This 100 μL solution almost completely eliminated the fluorescence. The fluorescent quenching of Fehling's solution is also confirmed by a video recorded under an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm). During the addition of Fehling's solution, the fluorescence gradually disappears. According to the results in Fig. 4(a) the consumption of the aldehyde group of CDs evokes fluorescence to almost completely disappear, by which the importance of the aldehyde of CDs for the fluorescence is stated.

Likewise, the fluorescence of CDs can be destroyed by NaHSO3 solution, as shown in Fig. 4(d). Gradually adding NaHSO3 solution results in the fluorescence decreasing. The inset of Fig. 4(d) shows that the fluorescence disappeared completely due to the addition reaction between the aldehyde of CDs and NaHSO3. The consumption of aldehyde of CDs leads to the fluorescent quenching, identical to the results recorded in Fig. 4(a) and (c).

Herein, the fluorescent quenching of CDs by aniline, NaHSO3 or Fehling's solution is due to the reactivity of the fluorophore of aldehyde in CDs. Addition reaction occurs between primary amino group of aniline or NaHSO3 and aldehyde of CDs. Therefore the aldehyde group is the vital fluorophore of CDs besides the CC bonds as is explained before.
In fact, the fluorescent lifetime of CDs is altered when quenched by KMnO4 or Fehling's solution, which demonstrated by analyses on Figs. S2 and S3 of Supporting Information.

It is interesting to find that the CDs prepared with citric acid and glycine have aldehyde groups, which are important fluorophores of CDs. Indeed, this is the first assurance of the fluorophore in CDs.

To summarize, the fluorescence of CDs come from the conjugate CC bonds and the aldehyde groups. In general, an individual aldehyde can not emit fluorescence, which can be proved by a large number of organic compounds. This indicates that the aldehyde groups participate in the conjugation formed by CC bonds, as depicted in Fig. 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses on the IR and X-ray photoelectron energy spectra of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is clear that the CC bonds and aldehyde groups are involved in the PL of CDs. The fluorophore is the basis for understanding the fluorescent mechanism of CDs and the fluorophore information can be provided by measuring IR spectra or XPS. However, due to the complex composition of CDs, IR spectrum provided mixed signals. The same functional group in different compounds does not provide the same signal. Other than that, some characteristic signals are adjacent in IR spectra. This causes peaks to overlap. Some key signals are, therefore, ignored in these situations. Moreover, the analyses of XPS data rely on multi-peak fitting; thereby some weak signals caused by key structures are possibly omitted. Given that, the analyses on the spectra of CDs should be more cautious.

The IR spectra of Fig. 5 (a) exhibit the characteristic peaks of CDs in the range of 2000-600 cm−1. The intensive peak at 1712 cm−1 should be attributed to the stretching vibration of CO of carboxyl whilst the shoulder peak at 1635 cm−1 should be caused by the overlapping stretching vibrations of CC and CO of aldehyde or ketone. The exact attribution of the peak value cannot be obtained, and IR cannot provide precise information about the structure. Fig. 5(b) shows the C 1s signals of CDs. Deconvolution is performed with different percentages of Gaussian and Lorentz functions, which represents four functional groups in CDs. Peak 284.7 eVis assigned to sp3–C of alkyl or sp2–C of CC double bonds. In this case, it is difficult to guarantee the CC double bond in CDs. Peak 285.4 eV is due to sp3-C of –CH2O– (–CH2OH or –CH2OCH2–). Obviously, 289.0 eV is attributable to –COOH derived from the starting material. The weak peak at 286.7 eV should belong to sp2–C of –CHO or CO. The elemental percentage of C in –CHO or CO of CDs is 13.4%. XPS of CDs indicates the presence of the aldehyde or ketone group. The oxidization of hydroxyl of citric acid may be a unique way to generate the aldehyde group. Herein, it is proved by the reaction with Fehling's solution. In my opinion, the analyses of these spectra should be combined with elaborate experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent response of CDs for dopamine or folic acid
ce:section.ce:para: Some CDs derived from carbohydrate were sensitive to biomolecules, such as dopamine [39]and folic acid [29]. The fluorescent quenching was employed in detecting the trace of them in aqueous medium. As for the quenching mechanism, some people attribute it to the electrostatic [40,41] or hydrogen bond [39] interaction between CDs and dopamine; some ascribe this to the formation of quantum dots-dopamine-quinone complex [32,42]; and some consider that the quenching should be due to the chemical reaction between dopamine and CDs [43]. In the process of folic acid detection, the quenching mechanism was attributed to the formation of complex between CDs and folic acid [29] through electrostatic interaction [41]. In our experiments, both dopamine and folic acid quenched the fluorescence of CDs prepared from citric acid and glycine. Fig. 6 indicates that the PL intensities of the CDs experience a graduate decline with the dropwise additions of dopamine or folic acid. The dilute dopamine results in a fixed wavelength of fluorescent descendant. However, slight red shifts occur after adding folic acid. Based in this result as well as the analyses above, we believe that the principle of this quenching should be based on the combination between aldehyde group of the CDs and the primary amino group of dopamine or folic acid. This combination leads to the consumption of aldehyde groups. For example, the fluorescence of the organic framework of alkaline earth metal ions with amino groups was quenched by gas phase aldehyde [44]. Similarly, the fluorescence of a conjugated polymer with primary amino groups was destroyed by glutaraldehyde [45]. These are due to the combination of aldehyde group and primary amino group. It can be seen that dropwise dopamine weakens the PL intensity of the CDs at the fixed emissions, further predicting the potential application of CDs in detecting dopamine.

Judging from the chemical structure of the raw materials, there is no aldehyde group at all but the aldehyde groups do exist in CDs and contribute to PL. Where does the aldehyde group come from? During the hydrothermal treatment process, amide or ester groups can be generated according to chemical properties of the raw materials. Hence, the oxidation of hydroxyl of citric acid is a unique way to produce aldehyde groups. So, the contribution of oxidation for the luminescence of CDs should not be ignored [46,47].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analyses on fluorescent quenching of CDs by Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: According to prior analyses, the aldehyde group of CDs serves a crucial role in luminescence. The interference of the aldehyde group may cause luminescence degradation. The oxygen atom in aldehyde had a double lone pair of electrons, which can be used as a coordination site with metallic cations such as Fe3+. The quenching phenomena caused by Fe3+ have been reported in some references [5,22,46,48]. Even if the raw materials used are diverse, it seems as an inherent property for CDs. Fe3+ with five semi-filled d orbitals accepted the excited electrons that reduce the radiative transition. Therefore, energy was transferred from the ligand to Fe3+ [49,50]. The fluorescent intensities of CDs are also obviously affected by Fe3+, as shown in Fig. 7 (a). With the addition of Fe3+, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched gradually. Finally, the fluorescence is almost entirely destroyed by 200 μL FeCl3 solutions. And, Fig. 7(b) shows that the absorption at 347 nm corresponding to n→π* transmission reduces dramatically by 6 μL Fe3+, which predicts the coordination of aldehyde group with Fe3+. The quenched fluorescence can be partially recovered by EDAT-Na4, see Fig. 7(c), because EDAT-Na4 possesses a stronger coordination with Fe3+. This declares that coordination belongs to a weak interaction [49]. Summarily, the fluorescence of CDs is sensitive to multiple analytes since they have several sorts of fluorophores.

Indeed, the similar fluorescent quenching still occurs on CDs derived from a sole citric acid [38], glucose or sucrose, as evidenced by the experimental results described in the Supporting Information. Fig. S4 and S5, S10 and S11 as well as S14 and S15 reveal that the fluorescence of these CDs can be destroyed by NaClO, KMnO4, aniline or Fehling's solution. CDs prepared from a sole citric acid are also sensitive to dopamine, folic acid or Fe3+, as shown in Figs. S7 and S8. Notably, when CDs were mixed with Fehling's solution and heated for a period of time, erythrine precipitation also occurs at the bottom of the test tubes, shown in Fig. S6, S12 and S16 respectively. These results prove that the CC bonds and the aldehyde groups in the CDs, even though the raw materials are diverse. All these similar results predict similar fluorophores of CDs.

In summary, the study show that NaClO, KMnO4, aniline, NaHSO3 or Fehling's solution can significantly eliminate the fluorescence emission of CDs. Based on experimental data, the destruction of aldehyde or CC bond groups leads to a rapid decay of the fluorescence, which reflects that the PL performance is originated from the CC bond and the aldehyde group of CDs. The fluorescence quenching of CDs is due to the reaction between fluorophore of CDs and analyte. Hence the quenching provides an efficient approach of seeking the fluorophore of CDs. This provides a practical way of researching fluorescent mechanism of CDs rather than depending only on analyses of IR spectrum or XPS since it provides mixed information about the chemical structure of CDs. However, an individual aldehyde group usually fails to cause fluorescence of a compound, the aldehyde groups in CDs should be conjugated to the CC bond, leading to a larger conjugation in CDs. In addition, the luminescent performances of the CDs prepared with diverse carbohydrates, such as sole citric acid, citric acid and glycine, glucose or sucrose, are rather similar. This indicates the common fluorophores in these CDs, which explains why it is by no means surprising that almost all CDs derived from carbohydrate had similar fluorescent emissions.

Peng Wu: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yuan Xiong: Investigation, Resources. Chen Lei: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis. Yuan Li: Methodology. Xiang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Cheng Zhang: Validation. Yudie Sun: Validation. Jian Zhang: Formal analysis. Kui Zhang: Project administration, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that there have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22106005, 21976002, 22004003) and Anhui Provincial Natural Science Foundation for Distinguished Young Scholars (2008085J11). This work acknowledges the fellowship award from the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong SAR, China (CityU PDFS2021-1S06).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2021.109750.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UV-Vis-NIR full-range responsive carbon dots with large multiphoton absorption cross sections and deep-red fluorescence at nucleoli and in vivo

UV-Vis-NIR Full-Range Responsive Carbon Dots with Large Multiphoton Absorption Cross Sections and Deep-Red Fluorescence at Nucleoli and In Vivo. Small 2020;16:2000680. http://dx.doi.org/10.1002/smll.202000680.
